request.question: Which arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose close living relatives are the wombats does Springwood State High School have?

title: Guan (bird)
Spotted elachura
Springwood State High School
Wakaleo vanderleuri
Koala
Rhagophthalmidae
Palorchestes
Centenary Heights State High School
Didunculus
Itatiella
sentences: The guans are a number of bird genera which make up the largest group in the family Cracidae.
They are found mainly in northern South America, southern Central America, and a few adjacent Caribbean islands.
There is also the peculiar horned guan ("Oreophasis derbianus") which is not a true guan, but a very distinct and ancient cracid with no close living relatives (Pereira "et al." 2002).
The spotted elachura or spotted wren-babbler ("Elachura formosa") is a species of passerine bird found in the forests of the eastern Himalayas and Southeast Asia.

In the past it was included in the babbler genus "Spelaeornis" as "S. formosus", but molecular phylogenetic studies in 2014 provided evidence that it was distinct from the babblers and part of a basal lineage (one that diverged early) with no other close living relatives within the passerine bird clade Passerida.
This led to the creation of a new family, Elachuridae, to accommodate just one species (a monotypic taxon).
Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.
The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.
Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.
In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Wakaleo vanderleuri is a species of marsupial lion that lived in Australia during the Miocene (about 16 to 10million years ago).
Being a marsupial, it is not closely related to true cats; its closest living relatives are wombats and the koala.
This diprotodont is hypothesized to have been an omnivore, eating significant amounts of meat, perhaps more like a bear in diet and habit than like an actual lion.
It lacked large canine-like fangs, producing some controversy regarding just how much it actually preyed on living animals.
The meat-eating hypothesis is based largely upon the morphology of its molar teeth.
The species is believed to have been able to hunt by leaping from trees onto its prey.
The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.
It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.
The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.
It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.
The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .
Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.
Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.
These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.
The Rhagophthalmidae are a family of beetles within the larger Elateroidea group that include click-beetles.

Members of this beetle family have bioluminescent organs on the larvae and are closely related to the Phengodidae (glowworm beetles) and Lampyridae (fireflies), and were often included in one of these families as a subfamily—Rhagophthalminae.
Towards the end of the 20th century, they were increasingly seen as a distinct family.
But they might be the only reasonably close living relatives of the mysterious fireflies of genus "Pterotus", and could eventually end up in a Lampyridae subfamily with these.
They were mainly included in the Phengodidae for having similar adaptation for displaying glowing lights.
However, this seems to be due to convergent evolution.

If they indeed belong in the Lampyridae, they are related to a group of families that have either a distinct type of flashing lights (Luciolinae) or rely exclusively or nearly so on pheromones for communication (the others).
Palorchestes ('ancient leaper or dancer') is an extinct genus of terrestrial herbivorous marsupial of the family Palorchestidae.
The genus was endemic to Australia, living from the Late Miocene subepoch through the Pleistocene epoch (around 11.6 mya – 11,000 years ago), and thought to be in existence for approximately .

Centenary Heights State High School is a State High School located in the suburb of Centenary Heights in Toowoomba, Queensland, with an enrolment of around 1500 students (2015) and was one of Queensland's leading high schools with 11 OP's 1 (Overall Position) scored in 2012.
The school is one of several state high schools in Toowoomba, including Toowoomba State High School , Harristown State High School and Wilsonton State High School.
The tooth-billed pigeons are the only genus ("Didunculus") of the subfamily Didunculinae, in the pigeon and dove family, (Columbidae).
It has no close living relatives, but it has been shown to be genetically close to the dodo, and the genus name "Didunculus" means "little dodo".

The jaw and tongue structure, and the superficially parrotlike bill have suggested a relationship to the parrots, but these features have arisen from its specialised diet rather than any real relationship.
Itatiella ulei is a species of moss in the family Polytrichaceae.
It is the only species in the genus Itatiella.
The Polytrichaceae is a common family of mosses that does not have close living relatives.
Its small size and the inflexed leaf apex characterize "Itatiella ulei".
When this species grows directly exposed to sun at high elevations, it presents a similar aspect but can be distinguished based on the distal lamella cells which are single and rhombic.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Which arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose close living relatives are the wombats does Springwood State High School have?
hop1_docs: Wakaleo | Wakaleo (indigenous Australian "waka", "little", "small", and Latin "leo", "lion") was a genus of medium-sized thylacoleonids that lived in Australia in the early to late Miocene.  It was approximately 2.5 ft (80 cm) long, or the size of a dog.  Although much smaller than its close relative, the marsupial lion "(Thylacoleo carnifex)", "Wakaleo" would have been a successful hunter in its time.  It had teeth specially designed for cutting and stabbing.  The ocelot-sized predator "Wakaleo", along with its jaguar-sized relative "Thylacoleo", were actually related to the herbivore wombats.

Koala | The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.  It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.  The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.  It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.  The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .  Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.  Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.  These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.

Wakaleo vanderleuri | Wakaleo vanderleuri is a species of marsupial lion that lived in Australia during the Miocene (about 16 to 10million years ago).  Being a marsupial, it is not closely related to true cats; its closest living relatives are wombats and the koala.  This diprotodont is hypothesized to have been an omnivore, eating significant amounts of meat, perhaps more like a bear in diet and habit than like an actual lion.  It lacked large canine-like fangs, producing some controversy regarding just how much it actually preyed on living animals.  The meat-eating hypothesis is based largely upon the morphology of its molar teeth.  The species is believed to have been able to hunt by leaping from trees onto its prey.

Springwood State High School | Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.  The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.  Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.  In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Muramura | "Muramura williamsi" is an extinct marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a dog, it was a herbivore.
Ngapakaldia | Ngapakaldia is an extinct genus of marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a sheep, it was a ground-dwelling herbivore.

Phalangeridae | The Phalangeridae are a family of nocturnal marsupials native to Australia, New Guinea and Eastern Indonesia, including the cuscuses, brushtail possums, and their close relatives.  Considered a type of possum, most species are arboreal, and they inhabit a wide range of forest habitats from alpine woodland to eucalypt forest and tropical jungle.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Springwood State High School: passage [4] — "home to many native animal species including Koalas".
- Koala (Phascolarctos cinereus): passage [2] — "arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia" and "closest living relatives are the wombats".

2) Ambiguities:
- The school text uses the plural "Koalas" but does not give a scientific name or detail (possible missing link to Phascolarctos cinereus).
- Several other marsupial taxa in the passages (Wakaleo, Muramura, Ngapakaldia) are related to wombats but are extinct or not described as arboreal herbivores; do not assume which page is intended without confirmation.
- Variant/common-name forms appear ("koala bear" in passage [2]) — check canonical species name.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as in the passages): "Koalas" (Springwood State High School page).
- Precise missing fact needed: confirmation that the school's "Koalas" are the species Phascolarctos cinereus (the arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose closest living relatives are wombats) and evidence that live koalas are present on campus.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "Springwood State High School" page — check the lede/infobox and sections titled Campus/Facilities/Biology/Fauna for the sentence about "Koalas", any links, and details on whether they are live animals on campus. (Search keywords: "Springwood State High School Koalas campus 'home to'".)
- Open "Koala" (Phascolarctos cinereus) page — check the lede and infobox for the exact description "arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia" and "closest living relatives are the wombats". (Search keywords: "Koala Phascolarctos cinereus lede infobox 'closest living relatives'".)
- If needed, open "Phascolarctos cinereus" or "Koala (disambiguation)" pages to confirm species naming/variants.

Final answer is deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and verified.
response.hop2_query: Springwood State High School koalas Phascolarctos cinereus campus
hop2_docs: Springwood State High School | Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.  The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.  Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.  In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.

Phascolarctos | Phascolarctos (from Ancient Greek "phaskolos", referring to a pouch or bag, and "arktos", meaning "bear") is a genus of marsupials containing only one extant species, the koala ("P. cinereus").  The genus was named by French zoologist Henri Marie Ducrotay de Blainville in 1816.

Giant koala | The giant koala ("Phascolarctos stirtoni") is an extinct arboreal marsupial which existed in Australia during the Pleistocene epoch.  "Phascolarctos stirtoni" was about one third larger than the contemporary koala, "P. cinereus", and has had an estimated weight of 29 lb , which is the same weight as a large contemporary male koala.  Although considered a part of the Australian megafauna, its body mass excludes it from most formal definitions of megafauna.  It is better described as a more robust koala, rather than a "giant"; in contrast, a number of Australian megafauna, such as "Diprotodon" and "Procoptodon goliah", were unambiguously "giants". The two koala species co-existed during the Pleistocene, occupying the same arboreal niche.  The reason for the extinction of the larger of the two about 50,000 years ago is unknown, although there are various hypotheses for the extinction.

Sporobolomyces koalae | Sporobolomyces koalae is a species of fungus in the order Sporidiobolales.  It is an anamorphic yeast.  Strains of the yeast were isolated from nasal swabs from three of five captive Queensland koalas ("Phascolarctos cinereus") kept at the Kobe Oji Zoo in Kobe, Japan.  Swabs from three zoo keepers were examined as well, but tested negative for the presence of the yeast.  It is not suspected to be pathogenic, as the koalas from which it was isolated were healthy.

Koala | The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.  It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.  The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.  It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.  The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .  Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.  Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.  These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.

Drop bear | A drop bear (sometimes dropbear) is a hoax in contemporary Australian folklore featuring a predatory, carnivorous version of the koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus").  This imaginary animal is commonly spoken about in tall tales designed to scare tourists.  While koalas are typically docile herbivores (and notably, not bears), drop bears are described as unusually large and vicious marsupials that inhabit treetops and attack unsuspecting people (or other prey) that walk beneath them by dropping onto their heads from above.

Durikainema | Durikainema is a genus of two nematodes in the family Robertdollfusidae.  Species have a head with a cuticular cephalic inflation, elongated papillae and amphids, and well-developed musculature.  Characteristics of the males include a single spicule and a long attenuated tail.  "Durikainema" species parasitize macropods.  The genus was circumscribed in 1982 with the type species "Durikainema macropi", a parasite of the eastern grey kangaroo ("Macropus giganteus"). " D. phascolarcti", parasite of the koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus"), was described in 1998.
response.summary_2: home to many native animal species including Koalas. Koala (Phascolarctos cinereus) — an arboreal herbivorous Australian marsupial; closest living relatives are the wombats.
